EDCS-00380551.translation: The century of Quintus Cornelius Verecundus.
EDCS-00380551.summary: This inscription records the military unit (century) commanded by Quintus Cornelius Verecundus.

EDCS-00380552.text_edition: Imperatore Traiano Hadriano Caesare Augusto II Gnaeo Fusco Salinatore consulibus pridie Idus Martias cum ventum esset in rem praesentem silvam Verlucionium arepennia decem quinque plus minus quod est in civitate Cantiacorum pago DIBVSSV RABIAS adfinibus heredibus et heredibus Caesenni Vitalis et via vicinale quod se emisse diceret Lucius Iulius Bellicus de Tito Valerio Silvino |(denariis) quadraginta sicut emptione continetur Lucius Bellicus testatus est se
EDCS-00380552.translation: When Imperator Trajan Hadrian Caesar Augustus was consul for the second time, and Gnaeus Fuscus Salinator was consul, the day before the Ides of March, as the matter came to hand, a wood called Verlucionium, about fifteen acres, which is in the territory of the Cantiaci, in the district DIBVSSV RABIAS, with the boundaries of the heirs and the heirs of Caesennius Vitalis and the vicinal road, which Lucius Iulius Bellicus said he had bought from Titus Valerius Silvinus for forty denarii, as contained in the bill of sale, Lucius Bellicus testified that he...
EDCS-00380552.summary: This inscription records a legal testimony by Lucius Iulius Bellicus concerning his purchase of a woodland property in the territory of the Cantiaci, dated to March 14, 118 AD.

EDCS-00380558.summary: This inscription is a formulaic blessing or wish, likely inscribed on an object, for a woman named Iuliane.

EDCS-00380559.summary: This inscription records the name of an individual, Aurelius Ursicinus, likely as a mark of ownership or dedication.

EDCS-00380560.summary: This inscription records the name of Aurelius Ursicinus in the genitive case, indicating possession or dedication.

EDCS-00380561.summary: This inscription records the name of Aurelius Ursicinus in the genitive case, indicating possession or dedication.

EDCS-00380563.summary: This inscription is a wish for a person named Eucherius to live, often found on funerary monuments or personal items.

EDCS-00380565.summary: This inscription is a wish for a person named Peregrinus to live, often found on funerary monuments or personal items.

EDCS-00380567.summary: This inscription likely records the name of Peregrinus in the genitive case, possibly indicating ownership or dedication, followed by an incomplete name or word.

EDCS-00380568.summary: This inscription is a wish for a person named Quisus to live, often found on funerary monuments or personal items.

EDCS-00380570.summary: This is a simple possessive inscription, likely indicating ownership or dedication to an individual named Silvicola.

EDCS-00380571.summary: This is a short acclamation or wish for long life, addressed to an individual named Silvicola, typical of a personal dedication or a funerary context.

EDCS-00380577.summary: This fragmentary inscription provides an incomplete personal name and an unclear second line, suggesting either a dedication or a personal mark.

EDCS-00380583.text_edition: SERADVASORISDVAS si servus si anciila si libertus liberta si mulier si baro popiam ferream EAENEC furtum fecerit domino Neptuno corulo parentatur
EDCS-00380583.translation: If a slave, male or female, if a freedman or freedwoman, if a woman, if a man, commits theft of an iron object from EAENEC, for this theft a sacrifice is to be made to Lord Neptune Corulus.
EDCS-00380583.summary: This inscription records a legal or religious decree specifying a penalty (a sacrifice to Neptune Corulus) for theft, regardless of the social status of the perpetrator, from a property likely belonging to EAENEC.

EDCS-00380587.summary: This is a signature inscription, indicating that Cravirius was the maker or creator of the object on which the text is inscribed.

EDCS-00380602.summary: This inscription presents two names, Icotasgus and possibly Amidda, which may indicate a dedication by or to these individuals, or a family relationship.

EDCS-00380614.translation: Paterclus made it, for Martinus, a decurion.
EDCS-00380614.summary: This inscription records that Paterclus made an object for Martinus, who held the rank of decurion, indicating a commission or dedication.

EDCS-00380616.text_edition: Nonis Martiis missi ad hospitium cum Marco medico faciendum structores numero XXX ad lapidem flammandum numero XVIIII ad lutum viminibus castrorum faciendum
EDCS-00380616.translation: On the Nones of March, sent to a lodging with Marcus the doctor: thirty builders for the making, nineteen for burning stone, for making mud with willow branches for the camp.
EDCS-00380616.summary: This inscription records a detailed administrative order from the Nones of March, dispatching specific numbers of skilled workers for various construction tasks related to a camp, including building, stone preparation, and mud production, alongside Marcus the doctor for lodging.

EDCS-00380617.text_edition: Felicioni |(centurioni) mutuo  XII Kalendas Iunias condimentorum |(sextarii) semissem |(denarii) |(quadrantem) halicae |(sextarii) semissem |(denarii) |(quadrantem) ova numero IIX |(denarii) |(quadrantem) XII Kalendas Iulias
EDCS-00380617.translation: To Felicio the centurion, as a loan: on the twelfth day before the Kalends of June, half a sextarius of spices, a quarter of a denarius; half a sextarius of pearl barley, a quarter of a denarius; eight eggs, a quarter of a denarius. On the twelfth day before the Kalends of July.
EDCS-00380617.summary: This inscription records a loan provided to Felicio, a centurion, detailing the specific quantities and values of various food items given on two distinct dates in June and July.

EDCS-00380618.text_edition: Eo magis me castigavit dum dicerem mercem nihil valere vel effunderem pro homine probo tuam maiestatem imploro ne patiaris me innocentem virgis castigatum esse et domine prout praefecto non potui queri qua valetudini detinebatur questus sum beneficiario  Frustra et centurionibus ceteris numeri eius proinde tuam misericordiam imploro ne patiaris me hominem transmarinarum et innocentem de cuius fide inquiras virgis cruentatum esse ac si aliquid sceleris comississem
EDCS-00380618.translation: He chastised me all the more because I said the merchandise was worth nothing, or because I poured it out. For a decent man, I implore your majesty not to allow me, an innocent man, to be chastised with rods; and Lord, just as I could not complain to the prefect because he was detained by ill health, I complained to the beneficiarius, in vain, and to the other centurions of his unit. Likewise, I implore your mercy not to allow me, a man from overseas and innocent, concerning whose loyalty you may inquire, to be bloodied with rods, as if I had committed some crime.
EDCS-00380618.summary: This inscription is a plea from an individual, a 'man from overseas,' to a higher authority, describing an unjust beating with rods for expressing dissatisfaction with merchandise, and appealing for intervention after failing to receive justice from his immediate superiors.

EDCS-00380623.text_edition: Maior Maritimo suo salutem scire te volui epistulas mihi missas esse ab patri sic meo in quibus scribit mihi ut ei notum faciam quid gessero defussa quod spica gessisti negotium cum Caesarianis fac ut certum mihi rescribas ut ego patri meo sic resscribere sic possim si quid inter numeraveris ego tibi sine mora Britem expellam pro ssumma quod efficiatur ego cum haec tibi scriberem lectum calfaciebam opto sis felicissimus salutat te frater vale  si puerum missurus es mittes chirografum cum eo quo securior sim  Vindolande Cocceiio Maritimo a Maiore
EDCS-00380623.translation: Maior to his Maritimus, greetings. I wanted you to know that letters have been sent to me by my father, in which he writes to me that I should inform him what I have accomplished. Regarding the business you conducted with the Caesariani concerning the defussa, make sure you write back to me so that I can write back to my father. If you have paid anything, I will immediately expel Britem for you. To ensure this is done, I was warming the bed when I wrote this to you. I hope you are very fortunate. Your brother greets you. Farewell. If you are going to send a boy, send a signed document with him so that I may be more secure. To Cocceius Maritimus at Vindolanda from Maior.
EDCS-00380623.summary: This is a private letter from Maior to Cocceius Maritimus in Vindolanda, discussing business matters, family correspondence, and personal well-being, likely dating to the early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-03000499.summary: This inscription records the name of Tiberius Claudius Catuarus, likely indicating ownership or dedication, and probably dates to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-03000500.text_edition: Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti  Britannicum ex argentariis Veb
EDCS-03000500.translation: Of Emperor Vespasian Augustus, Britannicus, from the silver-mines of Veb.
EDCS-03000500.summary: This inscription records a product or item from the silver-mines of Veb, named Britannicus, and identifies its origin under the reign of Emperor Vespasian Augustus (69-79 AD).

EDCS-03000501.text_edition: Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti  Britannicum ex argentariis Veb  Lucius R Adiutor
EDCS-03000501.translation: Of Emperor Vespasian Augustus, Britannicus, from the silver-mines of Veb. Lucius R. Adiutor.
EDCS-03000501.summary: This inscription records a product or item from the silver-mines of Veb, named Britannicus, identifying its origin under Emperor Vespasian Augustus (69-79 AD) and mentioning Lucius R. Adiutor, possibly a supervisor or owner.

EDCS-03000502.text_edition: Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti  Britannicum ex argentariis Veb  Lucius R Adiutor
EDCS-03000502.translation: Of Emperor Vespasian Augustus, Britannicus, from the silver-mines of Veb. Lucius R. Adiutor.
EDCS-03000502.summary: This inscription records a product or item from the silver-mines of Veb, named Britannicus, identifying its origin under Emperor Vespasian Augustus (69-79 AD) and mentioning Lucius R. Adiutor, likely a supervisor or owner.

EDCS-03000503.summary: This inscription identifies a person or object as being 'Augusta of the Thracians,' possibly referring to an empress or a person of high status associated with a Thracian unit or region, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-03000511.summary: This inscription records the abbreviated name Publius P. R., likely identifying an individual, and probably dates to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-03000519.translation: Emperor Carausius. Peace of the Emperor.
EDCS-03000519.summary: This inscription invokes Emperor Carausius and the 'Peace of the Emperor,' likely a coin legend or a public dedication dating to Carausius's reign (286-293 AD).

EDCS-03000521.summary: This inscription records the abbreviated name of Titus Flavius T., likely identifying an individual, and probably dates to the Flavian dynasty or later in the imperial period.

EDCS-03000523.summary: This fragment likely refers to a Colonia Claudia Victricensis, a Roman colony, and would have been part of a larger dedication or monument, probably dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-03000525.text_edition: Matrix tibi dico sede in tuo loco VO--S dedit tibi adiuro te per Ia et per Saba et per Adnai ne latus teneas sed sede in tuo loco nec noceas Cleuomedem filiam A
EDCS-03000525.translation: I say to you, O womb, sit in your place! V.O.--S gave it to you. I adjure you by Ia and by Saba and by Adnai, do not take to the side, but sit in your place and do not harm Cleuomedes, daughter of A.
EDCS-03000525.summary: This is a magical defixio, likely an uterium amulet, intended to compel a woman's womb to remain in place and prevent harm to Cleuomedes, probably dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-03000526.summary: This inscription records the military century of Ulpius Reginus, likely marking a building or property assigned to his unit.

EDCS-03300607.text_edition: Deo Mercurio Mintla Rufus donavi eos vel mulier vel PARIVSLIIFASPATEM materiam sagi donavi
EDCS-03300607.translation: To the god Mercury. Mintla Rufus donated these, either a woman or PARIVSLIIFASPATEM, I donated the material for a cloak.
EDCS-03300607.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Mintla Rufus to the god Mercury, involving a donation of some unspecified items or perhaps a person, along with material for a cloak.

EDCS-03300609.text_edition: Aunillus Vicariana Covitius Mini donat Varicillum Minura Atavacum
EDCS-03300609.translation: Aunillus Vicariana Covitius Mini gives Varicillus Minura Atavacus.
EDCS-03300609.summary: This inscription records a gift or dedication by Aunillus Vicariana Covitius Mini of a person named Varicillus Minura Atavacus, suggesting a form of personal offering or transfer.

EDCS-03300610.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dedication or record of individuals, listing Lucilia Mellossi and Minuassus Senebellenae, with an unclear intermediate word.

EDCS-03300630.text_edition: Sumtuaria --- Iunius --- |(denarios) IIII  Maternus sumtuaria  Bellettus
EDCS-03300630.translation: Sumtuaria --- Iunius --- 4 denarii. Maternus sumtuaria Bellettus.
EDCS-03300630.summary: This inscription appears to be an accounting record or ledger, detailing expenses or contributions, possibly involving two individuals named Iunius and Maternus, and a sum of four denarii.

EDCS-03300634.translation: The century of Titianus gave a cough.
EDCS-03300634.summary: This inscription enigmatically states that the century of Titianus 'gave a cough,' likely an idiomatic expression or a record of a specific event within a military unit.

EDCS-03700500.text_edition: SAS VAS Fronto II Tertius II Semilicinus II Sentius I Agilis I Fortunatus I VIIII
EDCS-03700500.translation: SAS VAS Fronto (two), Tertius (two), Semilicinus (two), Sentius (one), Agilis (one), Fortunatus (one). Total: nine.
EDCS-03700500.summary: This inscription appears to be a roster or tally, possibly of individuals or items, listing names with associated numbers and a total sum.

EDCS-03700506.summary: This inscription identifies the military unit known as the century of Niger, likely a simple marker or record of possession.

EDCS-03700512.text_edition: Nomine Camulorigis et Titocunae molam quam perdederunt sic in fanum sic dei devovi cuicumque numen sic involasit sic molam illam ut sanguinem suum mittat usque diem quo moriatur quicumque involasit! furta moriatur et PAVLATORIAM quicumque illam involasit sic et ipse moriatur quicumque illam involasit sic et VERTOGN de hospitio vel vissacio sic quicumque illam involasit sic a devo sic moriatur
EDCS-03700512.translation: In the name of Camulorix and Titocuna, the millstone which they lost, thus into the shrine, thus I have devoted to the god. Whoever has stolen it, may he shed his blood until the day he dies. Whoever has stolen it, may he die by theft, and by PAVLA. Whoever has stolen it, may he thus die. Whoever has stolen it, thus and VERTOGN from the guest house or inn. Thus, whoever has stolen it, thus from the god, thus may he die.
EDCS-03700512.summary: This is a curse tablet, or defixio, dedicated to a deity by Camulorix and Titocuna, invoking divine punishment, including death, upon anyone who stole their millstone, likely from a sacred context or inn.

EDCS-03700514.translation: The pottery of Placidus, from Marsia.
EDCS-03700514.summary: This inscription records the name of a pottery workshop and its owner, Placidus, identified as being from Marsia.

EDCS-03700516.text_edition: Corduba X pondo XCCL N CIA pondo CCXXII receptum accepit Auctinus OMA
EDCS-03700516.translation: Corduba: 10 pounds (weight), 275 [units of capacity?]. N.C.I.A.: 222 pounds (weight). Auctinus OMA received it as accepted.
EDCS-03700516.summary: This inscription appears to be an administrative record detailing received quantities of goods from Corduba and another source, noted as accepted by Auctinus OMA.

EDCS-03700517.text_edition: Votum Fortunae libens meruit meruXXX sic
EDCS-03700517.translation: A vow to Fortuna, willingly deserved, earned... so.
EDCS-03700517.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the goddess Fortuna, indicating that the dedicant willingly fulfilled a vow they felt was deserved.

EDCS-04900613.summary: This inscription presents two alternative names, Martia or Martina, likely referring to the same individual.

EDCS-04900617.text_edition: Deo sancto Mercurio Honoratus conqueror numini tuo me perdidisse rotas duas et vaccas quattuor et resculas plurimas de hospitiolo meo rogaverim genium numinis tuui sic ut ei qui mihi fraudem fecerit sanitatem ei non permittas nec iacere nec sedere nec bibere nec manducare si baro si mulier si puer si puella si servus si liber nissi sic meam rem ad me pertulerit et meam concordiam habuerit iteratis praecibus sic rogo numen tuum ut petitio mea statim pareat me vindicatum esse a maiestate tua
EDCS-04900617.translation: To the holy god Mercury, I, Honoratus, complain to your divinity that I have lost two wheels, four cows, and many small things from my guesthouse. I pray to the genius of your divinity that you not permit health to him who committed this fraud against me, nor permit him to lie down, nor to sit, nor to drink, nor to eat—whether man, woman, boy, girl, slave, or free—unless he returns my property to me and receives my forgiveness. With repeated prayers, I ask your divinity that my petition be immediately granted, that I be avenged by your majesty.
EDCS-04900617.summary: This is a curse tablet, or defixio, by Honoratus, appealing to the god Mercury to bring suffering upon the thief who stole property from his guesthouse until the stolen goods are returned.

EDCS-04900618.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely identifying an individual as the son of Titus.

EDCS-04900627.text_edition: Dis Manibus Aelia Sentica vixit annos XXXV Aurelius Verulus coniuci sic karissimae
EDCS-04900627.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aelia Sentica lived 35 years. Aurelius Verulus [made this] for his most beloved wife.
EDCS-04900627.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aurelius Verulus to his dearly beloved wife, Aelia Sentica, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-04900630.text_edition: Imperatore Domitiano    Imperatore Domitiano VIIII consule VII Idus Novembres Quintus Cassius Secundus miles legionis XX centuria Calvi Prisci scribsi me debere Gaio Geminio Mansueto militi legionis eiusdem centuria Vetti Proculi denarios centum quos
EDCS-04900630.translation: During the imperium of Domitian. During the ninth consulship of the emperor Domitian, on the 7th Ides of November, Quintus Cassius Secundus, soldier of the Twentieth Legion, in the centuria of Calvus Priscus, wrote that I owe Gaius Geminius Mansuetus, soldier of the same legion, in the centuria of Vettius Proculus, one hundred denarii, which...
EDCS-04900630.summary: This is a legal document, dated to the reign of Emperor Domitian, recording a debt of one hundred denarii owed by Quintus Cassius Secundus, a soldier of the Twentieth Legion, to Gaius Geminius Mansuetus, a soldier of the same legion.

EDCS-04900641.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription preserving the name Aurelius and a likely restoration of Victor.

EDCS-05000531.summary: This inscription records the name Marcus Ulpius, likely Maximus, along with his military rank as primus pilus, the senior centurion of a legion.

EDCS-05000532.text_edition: Olivarum albarum CCL Caio L A  Averni
EDCS-05000532.translation: 250 white olives for Gaius L. A. Avernus.
EDCS-05000532.summary: This inscription records a quantity of white olives likely as an offering or dedication to Gaius L. A. Avernus, possibly on a funerary monument or an offering tablet.

EDCS-05000534.text_edition: Luci Iuli Salutaris penicillum ad lippitudinem  Marini caesarianum
EDCS-05000534.translation: Lucius Julius Salutaris's brush for inflamed eyes. Marinus's Caesarian (remedy).
EDCS-05000534.summary: This inscription likely details a medical implement or remedy, specifically a brush for eye inflammation, attributed to Lucius Julius Salutaris and a 'Caesarian' remedy by Marinus, suggesting it was part of a medical kit or a practitioner's grave goods.

EDCS-05000537.translation: Of Publius Anicius Sedatus [three missing characters].
EDCS-05000537.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely funerary or dedicatory, belonging to Publius Anicius Sedatus, with the remainder of the text missing.

EDCS-05000539.summary: This inscription identifies a centurion named Lucius I. L., likely a military dedication or an epitaph.

EDCS-05000545.summary: This inscription indicates a military unit, specifically the century commanded by an individual named Sura, likely marking a property, a military camp, or a monument related to his unit.

EDCS-05000548.text_edition: Legionis II Augustae  Titi A C |(centurionis)
EDCS-05000548.translation: Of the Second Augustan Legion, of Titus A. C., centurion.
EDCS-05000548.summary: This inscription identifies a centurion, Titus A. C., belonging to the Legio II Augusta, likely from a military dedication, a building inscription, or his tombstone.

EDCS-05000550.translation: Of the Imperial Horse Guard, of Magnus.
EDCS-05000550.summary: This inscription refers to a member of the Equites Singulares Augusti (Imperial Horse Guard), specifically Magnus, likely a funerary monument or a dedication from or to him.

EDCS-05000551.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely funerary or dedicatory, pertaining to Quintus Valerius L., with the rest of his name or additional information missing.

EDCS-05000554.translation: [Three missing characters] Albano, perfume seller [missing text].
EDCS-05000554.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions a perfumer (seplasiarius) named Albanus, suggesting a commercial context, a funerary monument, or a dedication associated with his trade.

EDCS-05000557.summary: This inscription denotes a military unit, specifically the century led by Claudius Agisus, likely appearing on a building, a boundary marker, or a monument associated with his command.

EDCS-05000558.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely indicating a military unit or a centurial dedication.

EDCS-05000559.text_edition: XV Kalendas Iunias numerus purus cohortis I Tungrorum cui praeest Iulius Verecundus praefectus DCCLII in is |(centuriones) VI ex eis absentes singulares legati XLVI officio Ferocis Coris CCCXXXVII in is |(centuriones) II Londinio |(centurio) vas ad  appadun VI in is |(centurio) I AC  allia VIIII in is |(centurio) I Cipendiatum XI  in a I XXXXV summa absentes CCCCLVI in is |(centuriones) V reliqui praesentes CCLXXXXVI in is |(centurio) I ex eis aegri XV volnerati sic VI lippientes X summa eorum XXXI reliqui valentes CCLXV in is |(centurio) I
EDCS-05000559.translation: On the 18th of May, the net strength of the First Cohort of Tungrians, commanded by Julius Verecundus, prefect, is 752 men; among them, 6 centurions. Of these, 46 are absent on special duty for the legate; 337 with the office of Ferox Coris, among them 2 centurions; in London, 1 centurion; to the apparatus, 6, among them 1 centurion; AC allia, 9, among them 1 centurion; Cipendiatum, 11; in a I, 45. The total number of absentees is 456, among them 5 centurions. The remaining 296 are present, among them 1 centurion. Of these, 15 are sick, 6 wounded thus, 10 with eye inflammation. Their total is 31. The remaining 265 are well, among them 1 centurion.
EDCS-05000559.summary: This is a military document, likely a daily or monthly report, detailing the strength and status (present, absent, sick, wounded) of the First Cohort of Tungrians under the command of Prefect Julius Verecundus, dated to May 18th.

EDCS-05000560.text_edition: Donatur deo Mercurio si quis involaverit CLAM icinnum nec non alia minutalia Tocitami si baro si mulier si puella si puer si ingenuus si servus non ante eum laset sic quam mimbra sic rapi manu diem mortis concrutiat sic eum quq[u]i securam sic nnoris involavit EAAEAPR nec non et ququi res pictorias involaverit
EDCS-05000560.translation: Dedicated to the god Mercury. If anyone has stolen CLAM, the sacred object, and also other small items of Tocitami, whether man or woman, whether girl or boy, whether free or slave, may they not rest before their limbs, thus, are seized by the hand of death, crushing them, thus, and whoever securely stole it, EAAEAPR, and also whoever stole the painted objects.
EDCS-05000560.summary: This is a curse tablet or a dedication to Mercury, invoking a severe punishment upon anyone who steals an offering or other items belonging to Tocitami.

EDCS-05200468.text_edition: Chrauttius Veldeio suo fratri contubernali antiquo plurimam salutem et rogo te Veldei frater miror quod mihi tot tempus nihil rescripsti a parentibus nostris si quid audieris aut QVOT M in quo numero sit et illum a me salutabiss sic verbis meis et Virilem veterinarium rogabis illum ut forficem  quam mihi promisit pretio mittas per aliquem de nostris et rogo te frater Virilis salutes a me Thuttenam sororem Velbuteium rescribas nobis cum  se habeat opto sis felicissimus vale  Londini Veldedeio equisioni consularis a Chrauttio fratre
EDCS-05200468.translation: Chrauttius to his brother and old comrade Veldeius, greatest greetings. And I ask you, brother Veldeius, I am surprised that for so long you have written nothing back to me. If you have heard anything from our parents, or QVOT M, in what number he is, and you will greet him from me with these words. And you will ask Virilis the veterinary doctor to send me the shears he promised me, for a price, through one of our people. And I ask you, brother Virilis, greet our sister Thuttena for me. Write back to us how Velbuteius is. I hope you are most fortunate. Farewell. In London, to Veldedeius, a consular groom, from his brother Chrauttius.
EDCS-05200468.summary: This is a private letter from Chrauttius to his brother Veldeius, a consular groom in London, inquiring about family, asking for a favor (sending shears), and conveying greetings, suggesting a late 1st or early 2nd century CE date due to its Vindolanda tablet context.

EDCS-05200469.text_edition: Octavius Candido fratri suo salutem a Marino Nervio pondo centum explicabo e quo tu de hac re scripseras ne mentionem mihi fecit aliquotiens tibi scripseram spicas me emisse prope modios quinque milia propter quod |(denarii) mihi necessari sunt nisi mittis me aliquit sic |(denariorum)  minime quingentos futurum est ut quod arrae dedi perdam |(denarios) circa trecentos et erubescam ita rogo quam primum aliquit sic |(denariorum) mi mitte Coria quae scribis esse Cataractonio scribe dentur mi et karrum de quo scribis et quit sic sit cum eo karro mi scribe iam illec sic cepissem nissi sic iumenta non curavi vexsare sic dum viae malae sunt vide cum Tertio de denariis VIII semis quos a Fatale accepit non illos mi accepto tulit  scito mae explesse coria CLXX et bracis excussi habeo modios CXI fac |(denarios) mi mittas ut possim spicam habere in excussorio iam autem si quit sic habui perexcussi contubernalis Fronti amici hic fuerat desiderabat coria ei adsignarem et ita |(denarios) daturus erat dixi ei coria intra Kalendas Martias daturum Idibus  Ianuariis constituerat se venturum nec intervenit nec curavit accipere cum haberet coria si pecuniam daret dabam ei Frontinium Iulium audio magno licere pro cori ratione quem hic comparavit |(denarios) quinos saluta Spectatum Ianuarium Firmum epistulas a Gleucone accepi vale  Vindolandae
EDCS-05200469.translation: Octavius to his brother Candidus, greetings. From Marinus Nervius, I will produce one hundred pounds, concerning which you wrote about this matter, he made no mention to me. I had written to you several times that I bought nearly five thousand modii of grain, because of which I need denarii. Unless you send me at least five hundred denarii, it will happen that I will lose the three hundred denarii I gave as earnest money and will be ashamed. So I ask you, send me some denarii as soon as possible. As for the hides you write are at Cataractonium, write that they be given to me, and about the wagon you write of, and what it is like with that wagon, write to me. I would have already taken them there, if I hadn't hesitated to trouble the pack animals while the roads are bad. See with Tertius about the eight and a half denarii he received from Fatalis; he did not credit them to me. Know that I have completed 170 hides and have threshed 111 modii of grain. Arrange to send me denarii so I can have grain in the threshing floor. Now, if I had anything threshed, my comrade Frontinus, a friend, was here and wanted me to assign him hides, and he was going to give denarii. I told him I would give the hides by the Kalends of March. He had arranged to come on the Ides of January, but he neither came nor bothered to receive them, although he could have had the hides if he paid the money. I hear that Frontinius Julius is selling hides for a high price, at five denarii per hide he acquired here. Greet Spectatus, Ianuarius, Firmus. I received letters from Gleuco. Farewell. At Vindolanda.
EDCS-05200469.summary: This is a private letter from Octavius to his brother Candidus at Vindolanda, discussing business matters related to grain and hides, financial needs, and complaints about a mutual acquaintance, providing a glimpse into Roman military supply and economic transactions in early 2nd century Britain.

EDCS-05500596.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Publia Valeria Maximina nnorum VI Oppia Valeria et Sextus Pompeius Capratinus filiae pientissimae faciendum curaverunt sit tibi terra levis
EDCS-05500596.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Publia Valeria Maximina, six years old. Oppia Valeria and Sextus Pompeius Capratinus took care to have this made for their most devoted daughter. May the earth be light upon you.
EDCS-05500596.summary: This is a funerary inscription, a tombstone dedicated by Oppia Valeria and Sextus Pompeius Capratinus to their beloved daughter, Publia Valeria Maximina, who died at the age of six.

EDCS-06100391.text_edition: Deo Mercurio Docilinus QVAENM Varianus et Peregrina et Sabinianus qui pecori meo dolum malum intulerunt et INT RR prolocuntur sic rogo te ut eos maximo leto adigas nec eis sanitatem nec somnum permittas nisi a te quod mihi administraverint redemerint
EDCS-06100391.translation: To the god Mercury. Docilinus. QVAENM. Varianus and Peregrina and Sabinianus who inflicted evil harm upon my flock and speak INT RR, I ask you to drive them to the greatest death and allow them neither health nor sleep until they redeem from you what they administered to me.
EDCS-06100391.summary: This is a curse tablet or a defixio dedicated to the god Mercury by Docilinus, requesting divine retribution upon Varianus, Peregrina, and Sabinianus for harming his flock.

EDCS-07000709.text_edition: Biccus dat Mercurio quidquid perdidit si vir si mascet ne meiat sic ne cacet ne loquatur ne dormiat ne vigilet nec salutem nec sanitatem nessi sic in templo Mercurii pertulerit ne conscientiam de perferat sic nessi sic me intercedente
EDCS-07000709.translation: Biccus gives to Mercury whatever he lost, whether man or beast. May he not urinate, may he not defecate, may he not speak, may he not sleep, may he not wake, may he have neither safety nor health, unless he has endured thus in the temple of Mercury, may he not endure consciousness unless I intercede.
EDCS-07000709.summary: This is a curse tablet or a magical inscription, likely a prayer for recovery of lost property, invoking Mercury to inflict discomfort and suffering upon the thief until the stolen items are returned or Biccus intercedes.

EDCS-07000710.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Lingonum equitata cui praeest Fulvius Felix praefectus
EDCS-07000710.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. The First Cohort of Lingones, cavalry, commanded by Fulvius Felix, prefect.
EDCS-07000710.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the First Cohort of Lingones, a cavalry unit, under the command of their prefect, Fulvius Felix.

EDCS-07000715.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely a dedication or a funerary epitaph, addressed to Dobis Julius Macrinus.

EDCS-07000717.translation: The century of Optatus Domitius of the Sixth Legion.
EDCS-07000717.summary: This is a military inscription, likely a century mark, recording the century commanded by Optatus Domitius of the Sixth Legion.

EDCS-07000719.translation: The century of Quintus Attius Cilo.
EDCS-07000719.summary: This is a military inscription, likely a century mark, recording the century commanded by Quintus Attius Cilo.

EDCS-07000720.text_edition: Deo Vinotono Silvano Augusto Titus Orbius Primianus praefectus cohortis I Thracum
EDCS-07000720.translation: To the God Vinotonus Silvanus Augustus, Titus Orbius Primianus, prefect of the First Cohort of Thracians, [dedicates this].
EDCS-07000720.summary: This is a religious dedication by Titus Orbius Primianus, a prefect, to the deity Vinotonus Silvanus Augustus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07400541.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, possibly religious in nature, mentioning the word 'Sanctus'.

EDCS-07400545.summary: This is a very brief personal name inscription, possibly an ownership mark or a simple dedication.

EDCS-07400549.text_edition: Tibi rogo Metunus ut me vendicas de iste sic numene sic me vendicas ante quod veniant dies novem rogo te Metunus ut tu mi vendicas ante quod veniant dies novem
EDCS-07400549.translation: I beg you, Metunus, to avenge me concerning that. So by your power, avenge me before nine days come. I ask you, Metunus, that you avenge me before nine days come.
EDCS-07400549.summary: This is a defixio (curse tablet) appealing to the deity Metunus for vengeance, requesting swift action within nine days.

EDCS-07400550.text_edition: ExsupeArantius#Exsuperantius#XUPARANTI SilvioElaE,#Silviola,#SILVIELE, SattavillaE,#Sattavilla,#SATAVILE, ExsupeAratus#Exsuperatus#XSPARATUS Silvicolae=E#Silvicolae#SILVICOLE Avitus Melusso datus pervici te=TIBI#te#TIBI Santinus Magetus APIDIMIS Antonius Sanctus Vassianus Varasius datus
EDCS-07400550.translation: Exsuperantius, Silviola, Sattavilla, Exsuperatus, Silvicola, Avitus, given to Melussus, I overcame you, Santinus Magetus APIDIMIS, Antonius Sanctus, Vassianus Varasius, given.
EDCS-07400550.summary: This appears to be a list of names, possibly related to a curse or magical text, with some terms indicating 'given' or 'overcame'.

EDCS-07400552.text_edition: Dis Manibus  decurionis coloniae EboracensisVVCVS Marcus Aurelius Irenaeus pientissime fecit
EDCS-07400552.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Of the decurion of the colony of Eboracum. Marcus Aurelius Irenaeus made this most piously.
EDCS-07400552.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead, erected by Marcus Aurelius Irenaeus in memory of a decurion of the colony of Eboracum.

EDCS-07400554.translation: Lucius C F, centurion of the Twentieth Legion.
EDCS-07400554.summary: This is a military inscription, likely a century mark or a personal record, identifying Lucius C F as a centurion of the Twentieth Legion.

EDCS-07400559.text_edition: Claudia Severa Lepidinae suae salutem III Idus Septembres soror ad diem sollemnem natalem meum rogo libenter facias uti venias ad nos iucundiorem mihi diem interventu tuo factura si s  Cerialem tuum saluta Aelius meus te et filios salutant  sperabo te soror vale soror anima mea ita valeam karissima et have  Sulpiciae Lepidinae Cerialis a Claudia Severa
EDCS-07400559.translation: Claudia Severa to her Lepidina, greetings. On the third day before the Ides of September, sister, for my birthday celebration, I ask you to kindly come to us, making the day more joyful for me by your presence. If you do, greet your Cerialis. My Aelius greets you and your sons. I will hope to see you, sister. Farewell, sister, my soul, may I be well, dearest, and goodbye. To Sulpicia Lepidina from Cerialis to Claudia Severa.
EDCS-07400559.summary: This is a personal letter written on a wooden tablet from Claudia Severa to Sulpicia Lepidina, inviting her to a birthday celebration and conveying greetings, dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-07600291.text_edition: Donatio diebus quo perit Butu resque quae  nec ante sanetate sic nec salute nesi sic quam in domo die  sanetate sic in domo dei
EDCS-07600291.translation: A donation on the days when Butus perishes, and the things that neither before were in good health, nor now are in safety, unless they are thus in the house on the day of health, thus in the house of God.
EDCS-07600291.summary: This inscription appears to record a donation or endowment, possibly to a religious institution, made in connection with the death of an individual named Butus and concerning the welfare of certain assets.

EDCS-07600292.text_edition: Legionis XX Valeriae victricis cohortis  centuria Corneli Crescentis
EDCS-07600292.translation: Of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix, of the cohort, the century of Cornelius Crescens.
EDCS-07600292.summary: This inscription is a military record, identifying the century of Cornelius Crescens within a specific cohort of the Legio XX Valeria Victrix, likely marking a property, building, or unit within a military camp.

EDCS-07600293.text_edition: Docimedis perdidit manicilia dua qui illas involvit ut mentes suas perdat et oculos suos in fano ubi destinat
EDCS-07600293.translation: Docimedis lost two bracelets; whoever stole them, may he lose his mind and his eyes in the sanctuary where he intends.
EDCS-07600293.summary: This inscription is a curse tablet (defixio) dedicated in a sanctuary by Docimedis, calling for harm upon the person who stole two of his bracelets.

EDCS-07600294.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Postumus Augustus G[...].
EDCS-07600294.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Roman Emperor Postumus, likely part of a larger honorific monument or building inscription, dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800228.text_edition: Ulpius Silvanus factus Arausione emeritus legionis II Augustae votum solvit
EDCS-07800228.translation: Ulpius Silvanus, made emeritus of the Second Augustan Legion at Arausio, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800228.summary: This inscription records Ulpius Silvanus, a veteran of the Legio II Augusta, fulfilling a vow after his retirement at Arausio.

EDCS-07800231.text_edition: Titus Egnatius Tyrannus defictus est et Publius Cicereius Felix defictus est  Titus Egnatius Tyrannus defictus est et Publius Cicereius Felix
EDCS-07800231.translation: Titus Egnatius Tyrannus has been cursed, and Publius Cicereius Felix has been cursed. Titus Egnatius Tyrannus has been cursed, and Publius Cicereius Felix.
EDCS-07800231.summary: This inscription is a curse tablet (defixio) targeting Titus Egnatius Tyrannus and Publius Cicereius Felix, aiming to inflict harm upon them.

EDCS-07800232.text_edition: Tretiam Mariam defigC#defig#DEFIC et illiEus#illius#ILLEUS vitam et mentem et memoriam et iocinera pulmones intermxixita fata cogitata memoriam sic=CI#sic#SCI non possitt loqui (quae) seIcreta#secreta#SICRETA sint neque SINIT amaEre#amare#AMERE possit neque   claudo
EDCS-07800232.translation: I curse Tretia Maria and her life and mind and memory and liver, lungs, confused fates, thoughts, memory, so that she cannot speak what secrets there may be, nor can she love, nor to the lame.
EDCS-07800232.summary: This inscription is a detailed curse tablet (defixio) targeting Tretia Maria, seeking to afflict her with a range of physical and mental torments, including the loss of speech, memory, and the ability to love.

EDCS-07800234.text_edition: Aulus Alfidius Pomptina Olussa ex testamento heres posuit nnum LXX natus Athenis hic situs est
EDCS-07800234.translation: Aulus Alfidius Pomptina Olussa, heir by testament, placed this. Born in Athens, he lived 70 years, and is buried here.
EDCS-07800234.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aulus Alfidius Pomptina Olussa, set up by his heir according to his will, noting his age, place of birth, and burial site.

EDCS-07800235.text_edition: Marco Aurelio Eucarpo filio pientissimo vixit nnos XV menses VI Aurelia Eucarpia mater possuit sic
EDCS-07800235.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Eucarpus, her most dutiful son, who lived 15 years and 6 months. Aurelia Eucarpia, his mother, placed this.
EDCS-07800235.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aurelia Eucarpia to her beloved son, Marcus Aurelius Eucarpus, who died at the age of 15 years and 6 months.

EDCS-07800236.text_edition: Dis Manibus Flavius Agricola miles legionis VI Victricis vixit annos XLII dies X Albia Faustina coniugi inconparabili faciendum curavit
EDCS-07800236.translation: To the Manes Gods. Flavius Agricola, a soldier of the Sixth Legion Victrix, lived 42 years and 10 days. Albia Faustina arranged for this to be made for her incomparable husband.
EDCS-07800236.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavius Agricola, a soldier of the Legio VI Victrix who lived for 42 years, set up by his wife Albia Faustina in his memory.

EDCS-07800238.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iulius Valens miles legionis XX Valeriae victricis annorum XL hic situs est curam agente Flavio Attio herede
EDCS-07800238.translation: To the Divine Manes. Julius Valens, a soldier of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix, 40 years old, lies here. Flavius Attius, his heir, arranged for this.
EDCS-07800238.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julius Valens, a soldier of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix, set up by his heir Flavius Attius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800239.text_edition: Dis Manibus Titi Licini Ascani vivus sibi fecit
EDCS-07800239.translation: To the Divine Manes. Titus Licinius Ascanius made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-07800239.summary: This is a funerary inscription made by Titus Licinius Ascanius for himself during his lifetime, a practice common in Roman epitaphs.

EDCS-07800241.text_edition: Memoriae Valeri Amandini Valeri Superventor et Marcellus patri fecerunt
EDCS-07800241.translation: To the memory of Valerius Amandinus. Valerius Superventor and Marcellus made this for their father.
EDCS-07800241.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument dedicated by Valerius Superventor and Marcellus to the memory of their father, Valerius Amandinus.

EDCS-07800242.text_edition: Dis Manibus Vivio Marciano |(centurione) legionis II Augustae Ianuaria Martina coniunx pientissima posuit memoriam
EDCS-07800242.translation: To the Divine Manes. Ianuaria Martina, his most devoted wife, placed this memorial for Vivius Marcianus, a centurion of the Second Augustan Legion.
EDCS-07800242.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument dedicated by Ianuaria Martina to her husband, Vivius Marcianus, a centurion of the Second Augustan Legion.

EDCS-07800244.text_edition: Dis Manibus r Luci filius Claudia Celsus speculator legionis II Augustae Antonius Dardanus Cursor Valerius Pudens s Probus speculatores legionis
EDCS-07800244.translation: To the Divine Manes. Lucius' son, Claudia Celsus, a scout of the Second Augustan Legion. Antonius Dardanus, Cursor, Valerius Pudens, and Probus, scouts of the legion.
EDCS-07800244.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary dedication, possibly for multiple individuals, primarily identifying scouts of the Second Augustan Legion, including Claudia Celsus.

EDCS-07800245.text_edition: Gaius Etruscus Atiae carissimae suae meritis eius
EDCS-07800245.translation: Gaius Etruscus (made this) for Atia, his dearest one, on account of her merits.
EDCS-07800245.summary: This is a dedication made by Gaius Etruscus to Atia, likely a funerary monument or a memorial acknowledging her virtues.

EDCS-07800246.text_edition: Dis Manibus Claudiae Martinae annorum XIX Anencletus provincialis coniugi pientissimae hic sita est
EDCS-07800246.translation: To the Divine Manes. Anencletus, a provincial, (made this) for his most devoted wife, Claudia Martina, 19 years old. She lies here.
EDCS-07800246.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Anencletus, identified as a provincial, to his devoted wife, Claudia Martina, who died at the age of 19.

EDCS-07800247.text_edition: Dis Manibus Grata Dagobiti filia annorum XL Solinus coniugi karissimae faciendum curavit
EDCS-07800247.translation: To the Divine Manes. Solinus took care to have this made for his dearest wife, Grata, daughter of Dagobitus, 40 years old.
EDCS-07800247.summary: This is a funerary inscription arranged by Solinus for his beloved wife, Grata, daughter of Dagobitus, who was 40 years old.

EDCS-07800251.summary: This entry, 'A plus minus Hellenic', is not a complete or translatable Roman inscription but rather an editorial note likely describing the character or script of a fragmentary piece.

EDCS-07800258.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription, likely funerary, mentioning a duration of 2 years, possibly indicating age or service, with the beginning of the text missing.

EDCS-07800268.translation: To Ulpia, his daughter, from her mother.
EDCS-07800268.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a mother's dedication to her daughter, Ulpia.

EDCS-07800300.text_edition: Neptuno aram Lucius Aufidius Pantera praefectus classis Britannicae
EDCS-07800300.translation: To Neptune, an altar. Lucius Aufidius Pantera, prefect of the British fleet.
EDCS-07800300.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to Neptune by Lucius Aufidius Pantera, a prefect of the British fleet.

EDCS-07800302.text_edition: Iuliae Augustae matri senatus et castrorum Marcus Sabinius Victor ob
EDCS-07800302.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of the Senate and of the camps. Marcus Sabinius Victor on account of...
EDCS-07800302.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Julia Augusta, likely the empress Livia, as 'mother of the Senate and of the camps', set up by Marcus Sabinius Victor.

EDCS-07800308.summary: This fragment appears to be part of an inscription, possibly mentioning a freedman (l(ibertus)).

EDCS-07800323.text_edition: Memoriae Flaviae Victorinae Titus Tammonius Victor coniunx posuit
EDCS-07800323.translation: To the memory of Flavia Victorina. Titus Tammonius Victor, her husband, set this up.
EDCS-07800323.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Flavia Victorina by her husband, Titus Tammonius Victor.

EDCS-07800324.text_edition: Matribus Italis Germanis Gallis Britannis Antonius Lucretianus beneficiarius consularis restituit
EDCS-07800324.translation: To the Italian, German, Gallic, and British Mothers, Antonius Lucretianus, a beneficiarius consularis, restored this.
EDCS-07800324.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a monument by Antonius Lucretianus, a consular beneficiarius, dedicated to a group of local 'Mothers' deities, including Italian, German, Gallic, and British forms.

EDCS-07800327.text_edition: Neptuno et Minervae templum pro salute domus divinae ex auctoritate Tiberi Claudi Cogidubni regis magni Britanniae collegium fabrorum et qui in eo sunt de suo dederunt donante aream ente Pudentini filio
EDCS-07800327.translation: To Neptune and Minerva, the college of craftsmen and those who are in it, gave this temple from their own funds, for the welfare of the divine house, by the authority of Tiberius Claudius Cogidubnus, great king of Britain, with the area being gifted by the son of Pudentinus.
EDCS-07800327.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple to Neptune and Minerva by a college of craftsmen for the welfare of the imperial house, authorized by the client king Tiberius Claudius Cogidubnus, with the land donated by the son of Pudentinus, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-07800330.text_edition: cca Aelia  Cauva  filia annorum XXXVI
EDCS-07800330.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelia Cauva, who died at the age of 36.

EDCS-07800331.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Catia Censorina, who died at the age of 23.

EDCS-07800336.text_edition: Deae Ancastae Geminus Mani votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800336.translation: To the Goddess Ancasta, Geminus Manius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800336.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Geminus Manius fulfilling a vow to the goddess Ancasta.

EDCS-07800337.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Carinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-07800337.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Carinus, likely a public monument from the late 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800339.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription with illegible text, making its original purpose and content impossible to determine.

EDCS-07800342.inscription_type: carmina, inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-07800342.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Septimius  vir perfectissimus praeses Britanniae primae restituit civis Remus  Signum et erectam prisca religione columnam  Septimius renovat primae provinciae rector
EDCS-07800342.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Lucius Septimius, a most perfect man, president of Britannia Prima, restored this as a citizen of Remi. Septimius, governor of the first province, renews the statue and the column erected by ancient religion.
EDCS-07800342.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a statue and column dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Septimius, a Roman governor, likely in the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-07800343.translation: To the god Silvanus. Sabidius Maximus.
EDCS-07800343.summary: This is a religious dedication to the god Silvanus by Sabidius Maximus, likely an altar or votive offering.

EDCS-07800344.text_edition: Sulevis Sulinus Bruceti votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800344.translation: To the Suleviae. Sulinus Bruceti willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800344.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Sulinus Bruceti to the Suleviae, likely a local or Celtic deity group.

EDCS-07800345.summary: This is a fragmentary religious dedication to the Suleviae by Primus, likely an altar or votive offering.

EDCS-07800347.text_edition: Dannicus eques alae Indianae turma Albani stipendiorum XVI cives Rauricus curaverunt Fulvius Natalis it Flavius Bitucus ex testamento hic situs est
EDCS-07800347.translation: Dannicus, a cavalryman of the Ala Indiana, of Albanus's troop, with 16 years of service, a Raurican citizen. Fulvius Natalis and Flavius Bitucus oversaw this according to his will. He lies here.
EDCS-07800347.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Dannicus, a cavalryman of the Ala Indiana, erected by his associates according to his will, likely dating from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-07800348.text_edition: Sextus Valerius Genialis eques alae Trhaecum civis Frisiavus turma Genialis annorum XXXX stipendiorum XX hic situs est heres faciendum curavit
EDCS-07800348.translation: Sextus Valerius Genialis, a cavalryman of the Ala Thracum, a Frisian citizen, of Genialis's troop, 40 years old, with 20 years of service, lies here. His heir had this made.
EDCS-07800348.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Valerius Genialis, a cavalryman, set up by his heir, likely dating from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-07800349.text_edition: Philus Cassavi filius civis Sequanus nnorum XXXXV hic situs est
EDCS-07800349.translation: Philus, son of Cassavus, a Sequanian citizen, 45 years old, lies here.
EDCS-07800349.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Philus, a Sequanian citizen, likely a private tombstone.

EDCS-07800350.text_edition: Dis Manibus Publiae Vicanae Publius Vitalis coniux
EDCS-07800350.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead of Publia Vicana. Publius Vitalis, her husband.
EDCS-07800350.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Publia Vicana by her husband, Publius Vitalis, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-07800351.text_edition: Dis Manibus Castae Castrensis vixit annos
EDCS-07800351.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Casta Castrensis. She lived for [---] years.
EDCS-07800351.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Casta Castrensis, likely a woman, indicating her name and age at death, though the age is missing.

EDCS-07800352.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iuliae Castae coniugi vixit nnos XXXIII
EDCS-07800352.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Iulia Casta, my wife. She lived for 33 years.
EDCS-07800352.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Casta, erected by her husband, detailing her name, marital status, and age at death.

EDCS-07800358.text_edition: Deo Genio cohortis cunctae Morivendus VV
EDCS-07800358.translation: To the god Genius of the entire cohort, Morivendus [made this] willingly, willingly.
EDCS-07800358.summary: This is a dedication to the Genius of a military cohort, made by an individual named Morivendus, likely as an act of piety or a vow fulfilled.

EDCS-07800360.text_edition: Rufus Sita eques cohortis VI Tracum nnorum XL stipendiorum XXII heredes exs testamento faciendum curaverunt hic situs est
EDCS-07800360.translation: Rufus Sita, a cavalryman of the 6th Cohort of Thracians, aged 40 years, with 22 years of service. His heirs, according to his will, arranged for this to be made. He is buried here.
EDCS-07800360.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Rufus Sita, a Thracian cavalryman, detailing his military unit, age, years of service, and indicating that his heirs erected the monument according to his will.

EDCS-07800361.text_edition: miles legionis XX centuria Livi Saturnini stipendiorum XIII annorum XXXX
EDCS-07800361.translation: A soldier of the Twentieth Legion, from the centuria of Livius Saturninus, with 13 years of service, aged 40 years.
EDCS-07800361.summary: This is a funerary inscription for an unnamed soldier of the Legio XX, specifying his military unit, centuria, years of service, and age at death.

EDCS-07800362.translation: She lived 20 years. Ingenuina, his wife.
EDCS-07800362.summary: This is likely a fragment of a funerary inscription for an unnamed individual, stating their age at death and identifying Ingenuina as their spouse, probably the dedicant.

EDCS-07800371.text_edition: Deo Romulo Gulioepius donavit Iuventinus fecit
EDCS-07800371.translation: To the god Romulus, Gulioepius donated this. Iuventinus made it.
EDCS-07800371.summary: This is a dedication to the god Romulus, indicating Gulioepius as the donor and Iuventinus as the craftsman of the monument.

EDCS-07800372.text_edition: Dis Manibus Iulia Ingenuilla vixit annos XX menses V dies XXVIIII
EDCS-07800372.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Iulia Ingenuilla. She lived 20 years, 5 months, 29 days.
EDCS-07800372.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Ingenuilla, detailing her name and precise age at death.

EDCS-07800373.text_edition: Dis Manibus Sulicena annos XIIII vixit
EDCS-07800373.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Sulicena. She lived 14 years.
EDCS-07800373.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sulicena, stating her name and age at death.

EDCS-07800374.translation: Iul[ius] willingly and deservedly paid/fulfilled [his vow].
EDCS-07800374.summary: This is a fragmentary votive inscription, likely by an individual named Julius, indicating a vow or offering made willingly and deservedly.

EDCS-07800375.text_edition: Dis Manibus Metti natione Geta vixit nnos XXXV heres posuit
EDCS-07800375.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Mettius, of the Getae nation, who lived 35 years. His heir placed this.
EDCS-07800375.summary: This is a funerary inscription, set up by an heir for Mettius, a man of Getae origin, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-07800376.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely pertains to an individual named Spes, associated with a Gaius Sentius, possibly as a slave or freedwoman.

EDCS-07800377.text_edition: Deae Dianae sacratissimae votum solvit Vettius Benignus libertus
EDCS-07800377.translation: Vettius Benignus, freedman, willingly fulfilled his vow to the most sacred goddess Diana.
EDCS-07800377.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Vettius Benignus, a freedman, who fulfilled a vow to the goddess Diana.

EDCS-07800378.text_edition: Genio loci IANP    legionis VI victricis Forianus votum solvit laetus libens merito
EDCS-07800378.translation: To the Genius of the place, [---] of the Sixth Legion Victrix, Forianus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, joyful.
EDCS-07800378.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Forianus, likely a soldier of the Sixth Legion Victrix, to the local Genius (tutelary spirit) of a place.

EDCS-07800379.text_edition: Peregrinus Secundi filius civis Trever Loucetio Marti et Nemetona votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800379.translation: Peregrinus, son of Secundus, a Trever citizen, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Mars Loucetius and Nemetona.
EDCS-07800379.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication made by Peregrinus, a Trever citizen, to the Celtic deities Mars Loucetius and Nemetona.

EDCS-07800382.text_edition: Claudius Ligur  ae nimia vetustate  colegio longa seria annorum  sua pecunia refici et repingi curavit  Gaius Protacius deae Sulis Minervae  EPEPAM
EDCS-07800382.translation: Claudius Ligur... due to great age... the college for a long series of years... saw to the repair and repainting at his own expense. Gaius Protacius to the goddess Sulis Minerva...
EDCS-07800382.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription detailing a restoration project undertaken by Claudius Ligur for a college, and also mentioning a dedication by Gaius Protacius to Sulis Minerva.

EDCS-07800385.text_edition: Deae Suli pro salute et incolumitate Marci Aufidi Maximi |(centurionis) legionis VI victricis Aufidius Eutuches lebertus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800385.translation: To the goddess Sulis, for the health and safety of Marcus Aufidius Maximus, centurion of the Sixth Legion Victrix, Aufidius Eutuches, freedman, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800385.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aufidius Eutuches, a freedman, to the goddess Sulis, for the well-being of Marcus Aufidius Maximus, a centurion of the Sixth Legion Victrix.

EDCS-07800386.text_edition: Deae Suli pro salute et incolumitate Aufidi Maximi |(centurionis) legionis VI victricis Marcus Aufidius Lemnus libertus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800386.translation: To the goddess Sulis, for the health and safety of Aufidius Maximus, centurion of the Sixth Legion Victrix, Marcus Aufidius Lemnus, freedman, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800386.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Marcus Aufidius Lemnus, a freedman, to the goddess Sulis, for the well-being of Aufidius Maximus, a centurion of the Sixth Legion Victrix.

EDCS-07800388.text_edition: Deae Suli Minervae et Numinibus Augustorum Gaius Curiatius Saturninus |(centurio) legionis II Augustae pro se suisque votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800388.translation: To the goddess Sulis Minerva and the Deities of the Emperors, Gaius Curiatius Saturninus, centurion of the Second Legion Augusta, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-07800388.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Gaius Curiatius Saturninus, a centurion of the Second Legion Augusta, to Sulis Minerva and the imperial cult for himself and his kin.

EDCS-07800389.text_edition: Deae Suli ob salutem sacrum Gai Iavoleni Saturnalis  imaginiferi legionis II Augustae Lucius Manius Dionisias sic libertus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800389.translation: To the goddess Sulis, for the safety of Gaius Iavolenus Saturnalis, standard-bearer of the Second Legion Augusta, Lucius Manius Dionisias, freedman, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his sacred vow.
EDCS-07800389.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Lucius Manius Dionisias, a freedman, to the goddess Sulis for the safety of Gaius Iavolenus Saturnalis, an imaginifer (standard-bearer) of the Second Legion Augusta.

EDCS-07800390.summary: This is a brief dedication by Quintus Pompeius Anicetus to the goddess Sulis.

EDCS-07800392.text_edition: Deae Suli Minervae Sulinus Maturi filius votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800392.translation: To the goddess Sulis Minerva, Sulinus, son of Maturus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800392.summary: This inscription records Sulinus, son of Maturus, dedicating a monument to Sulis Minerva as a fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-07800393.text_edition: Sulevis Sulinus scultor Bruceti filius sacrum fecit libens merito
EDCS-07800393.translation: To the Suleviae, Sulinus, the sculptor, son of Brucetus, willingly and deservedly made this sacred offering.
EDCS-07800393.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication by Sulinus the sculptor, son of Brucetus, to the Suleviae, a group of Celtic goddesses, during the Roman period.

EDCS-07800394.text_edition: Locum religiosum per insolentiam erutum virtuti et numini Augusti repurgatum reddidit Gaius Severius Emeritus regionarius
EDCS-07800394.translation: Gaius Severius Emeritus, a regional officer, restored and purified this sacred place, which had been disturbed by insolence, to the virtue and divinity of Augustus.
EDCS-07800394.summary: This inscription records the restoration and purification of a sacred site by Gaius Severius Emeritus, a regional officer, dedicated to Emperor Augustus, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-07800395.text_edition: Novanti filius pro se et suis ex visu possuit sic
EDCS-07800395.translation: The son of Novantius placed this for himself and his family, according to a vision, thus.
EDCS-07800395.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Novantius' son for himself and his family, prompted by a vision.

EDCS-07800396.text_edition: Qui mihi VILBIAM involavit sic liquat sic comodo sic aqua ella sic muta qui eam voravit si Velvinna Exsupereus Verianus Severinus Augustalis Comitianus Catusminianus Germanilla Iovina
EDCS-07800396.translation: Whoever stole VILBIA from me, thus may they melt, thus may they be convenient, thus may the water be, thus may she be silent who devoured her, whether Velvinna, Exsupereus, Verianus, Severinus, Augustalis, Comitianus, Catusminianus, Germanilla, Iovina.
EDCS-07800396.summary: This is a curse tablet or defixio, invoking misfortune upon those who stole an item named VILBIA, listing several potential perpetrators, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-07800397.text_edition: Dis Manibus Caius Calpurnius Receptus sacerdos deae Sulis vixit annos LXXV Calpurnia Trifosa liberta coniunx faciendum curavit
EDCS-07800397.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Calpurnius Receptus, priest of the goddess Sulis, lived 75 years. Calpurnia Trifosa, his freedwoman wife, had this made.
EDCS-07800397.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Calpurnius Receptus, a priest of Sulis, erected by his freedwoman wife Calpurnia Trifosa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800398.text_edition: Iulius Vitalis fabriciensis legionis XX Valeriae victricis stipendiorum IX annorum XXIX natione Belga ex collegio fabricensium elatus hic situs est
EDCS-07800398.translation: Here lies Julius Vitalis, a craftsman of the Twentieth Valeria Victrix Legion, who served for 9 years, lived 29 years, of Belgian origin, buried by the guild of craftsmen.
EDCS-07800398.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julius Vitalis, a craftsman (fabriciensis) of Legio XX Valeria Victrix, who was of Belgian origin and was buried by his guild, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800399.text_edition: Gaius Murrius Gai filius Arniensis Foro Iuli Modestus miles legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis centuria Iuli Secundi nnorum XXV stipendiorum  hic situs est
EDCS-07800399.translation: Here lies Gaius Murrius, son of Gaius, of the Arnian tribe from Forum Iuli, Modestus, a soldier of the Second Adiutrix Pia Fidelis Legion, in the century of Iulius Secundus, 25 years old, with [---] years of service.
EDCS-07800399.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Murrius Modestus, a Roman soldier from Forum Iuli serving in Legio II Adiutrix, placed at his burial site, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800400.text_edition: Dis Manibus Marcus Valerius Marci filius Latinus civis Equester miles legionis XX annorum XXXV stipendiorum XX hic situs est
EDCS-07800400.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Here lies Marcus Valerius Latinus, son of Marcus, an equestrian citizen, a soldier of the Twentieth Legion, 35 years old, with 20 years of service.
EDCS-07800400.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Valerius Latinus, an equestrian citizen and soldier of Legio XX, who lived 35 years and served for 20, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800401.text_edition: Lucius Vitellius Mantai filius Tancinus cives Hispanus Cauriensis eques alae Vettonum civium Romanorum nnorum XXXXVI stipendiorum XXVI hic situs est
EDCS-07800401.translation: Lucius Vitellius Tancinus, son of Mantai, a Spanish citizen from Caurium, a horseman of the Ala Vettonum of Roman citizens, aged 46, with 26 years of service, lies here.
EDCS-07800401.summary: This is a tombstone for a Roman auxiliary cavalryman from Spain, recording his name, origin, unit, age, and years of service, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-07800402.text_edition: Sergia Antigonus Nicopoli emeritus ex legione XX annorum XLV hic situs est Gaius Tiberinus heres faciendum curavit
EDCS-07800402.translation: Sergia Antigonus from Nicopolis, a veteran discharged from the Twentieth Legion, aged 45, lies here. Gaius Tiberinus, his heir, had this monument made.
EDCS-07800402.summary: This epitaph commemorates a discharged veteran of Legio XX, Sergia Antigonus, with his age and origin, erected by his heir Gaius Tiberinus, probably from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-07800403.text_edition: decurio coloniae Glevensis  vixit annos LXXX VI
EDCS-07800403.translation: Decurion of the colony of Glevum, lived 86 years.
EDCS-07800403.summary: This fragmented inscription records the age and civil status of an individual, likely a prominent citizen of the Roman colony of Glevum (Gloucester), who served as a decurion, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07800404.text_edition: Dis Manibus Merc Magni liberta alumna vixit annum I menses VI dies XII
EDCS-07800404.translation: To the Divine Manes. Merc, a freedwoman and foster-child of Magnus, lived 1 year, 6 months, 12 days.
EDCS-07800404.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the dead for a very young girl named Merc, identified as a freedwoman and foster-child of Magnus, recording her very short lifespan, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07800405.text_edition: Rusoniae Aventinae civi Mediomatricae nnum LVIII hic sita est Lucius Ulpius Sestius heres faciendum curavit
EDCS-07800405.translation: For Rusonia Aventina, a citizen of Mediomatrici, aged 58, lies here. Lucius Ulpius Sestius, her heir, had this made.
EDCS-07800405.summary: This funerary monument records the name, origin, and age of Rusonia Aventina, a citizen from the Mediomatrici tribe, erected by her heir Lucius Ulpius Sestius, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07800406.text_edition: Dis Manibus Successae Petroniae vixit nnos III menses IIII dies IX Vettius Romulus et Victoria Sabina filiae karissimae fecerunt
EDCS-07800406.translation: To the Divine Manes. For Successa Petronia, who lived 3 years, 4 months, 9 days. Vettius Romulus and Victoria Sabina made this for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-07800406.summary: This is a dedicatory epitaph to the spirits of the dead, erected by Vettius Romulus and Victoria Sabina for their beloved young daughter, Successa Petronia, noting her brief life, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07800407.text_edition: Vibia Iucunda annorum XXX hic sepulta est
EDCS-07800407.translation: Vibia Iucunda, aged 30, is buried here.
EDCS-07800407.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription for Vibia Iucunda, stating her age and place of burial, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800411.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely from a tombstone, mentioning a man's name ending in -ilius and his wife, dating is uncertain due to its incomplete nature.

EDCS-07800412.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, preserving only the end of a name, likely part of a longer text such as an epitaph or dedication, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-07800415.translation: ...[?]nus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800415.summary: This is a fragment from a dedicatory inscription, indicating that an individual (whose name ends in -nus) fulfilled a vow as a result of a divine favor, typical of a votive altar or tablet, probably from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07800421.text_edition: Pro salute imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti Naevius Augusti libertus adiutor procuratorum principia ruina oppressa a solo restituit
EDCS-07800421.translation: For the health of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Naevius, freedman of Augustus, assistant to the procurators, restored from the ground up the principia (headquarters) which had been crushed by ruin.
EDCS-07800421.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a military headquarters building by Naevius, an imperial freedman, dedicated to the health of Emperor Caracalla (c. 211-217 CE).

EDCS-07800422.translation: Anus founded this, in the consulship of [---] and Quintianus.
EDCS-07800422.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a foundation by a person named Anus, dated to a specific consulship (likely 235 CE, with Quintianus as one of the consuls).

EDCS-07800423.text_edition: Numinibus divorum Augustorum Caius Indutius Felix Silvano votum solvit libens merito  CON VIC GA
EDCS-07800423.translation: To the Deities of the Divine Augusti, Gaius Indutius Felix willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus, in the vicus of Gariannonum.
EDCS-07800423.summary: This inscription is a votive offering by Gaius Indutius Felix to the divinized emperors and the god Silvanus, likely set up in the vicus Gariannonum (present-day Ribchester, UK).

EDCS-07800429.text_edition: Deo Marti Rigisamo Iventius Sabinus votum solvit libens laetus merito
EDCS-07800429.translation: To the god Mars Rigisamus, Iuventius Sabinus willingly, gladly, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800429.summary: This inscription is a votive offering by Iuventius Sabinus to the local Celtic-Roman deity Mars Rigisamus.

EDCS-07800433.text_edition: Deo Marti Medocio Campesium et Victorie Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti nostri donum Lossio Veda de suo posuit nepos Vepogeni Caledo
EDCS-07800433.translation: To the god Mars Medocius Campesius and the Victory of our Emperor Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, Lossio Veda, grandson of Vepogenus Caledo, placed this gift from his own funds.
EDCS-07800433.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Lossio Veda to the local deities Mars Medocius Campesius and the personified Victory of Emperor Severus Alexander (222-235 CE).

EDCS-07800434.text_edition: Matribus Sulevis Similis Atti filius civis Cantiacus votum libens solvit
EDCS-07800434.translation: To the Mother Goddesses Suleviae, Similis, son of Attus, a citizen of the Cantiaci, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800434.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication by Similis, a member of the Cantiaci tribe, to the Matres Suleviae, a group of Celtic mother goddesses.

EDCS-07800435.text_edition: Numinibus Augustorum et Mercurio deo Andescociuouco Imilico Aesurilini libertus aram opere maronio de suo dedit
EDCS-07800435.translation: To the Deities of the Augusti and the god Mercury Andescociuoucus, Imilico, freedman of Aesurilinus, dedicated an altar made of marble from his own funds.
EDCS-07800435.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a marble altar by Imilico, an imperial freedman, to the Numen of the Augusti and the Romano-Celtic god Mercury Andescociuoucus.

EDCS-07800436.text_edition: Deo Silvano Callirio donum Cintusmus aerarius votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800436.translation: To the god Silvanus Callirius, Cintusmus, a bronzesmith, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800436.summary: This inscription is a votive offering by Cintusmus, a bronzesmith, to the god Silvanus Callirius.

EDCS-07800437.text_edition: Deo Silvano Hermes votum solvit libens vovit
EDCS-07800437.translation: To the god Silvanus, Hermes willingly fulfilled his vow and vowed this.
EDCS-07800437.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication by Hermes to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-07800442.text_edition: Marcus Favonius Marci filius Pollia Facilis |(centurio) legionis XX Verecundus et Novicius liberti posuerunt hic situs est
EDCS-07800442.translation: Marcus Favonius Facilis, son of Marcus, of the Pollian tribe, centurion of the Twentieth Legion, and his freedmen Verecundus and Novicius, placed this; he is interred here.
EDCS-07800442.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Favonius Facilis, a centurion of Legio XX, placed by himself and his freedmen Verecundus and Novicius.

EDCS-07800443.text_edition: Longinus Sdapezematygi filius duplicarius ala prima Thracum pago Sardica nnorum XL aerorum XV heres exs sic testamento faciendum curavit hic situs est
EDCS-07800443.translation: Longinus, son of Sdapezematygus, a duplicarius in the first Thracian ala, from the district of Sardica, 40 years old, 15 years in service. His heir, according to his will, had this made; he is laid here.
EDCS-07800443.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Longinus, a cavalryman from the first Thracian ala, erected by his heir as per his will, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-07800444.text_edition: O  Macri us eques Romanus vixit annos XX Valeria Frontina coniuux et Florius Cogitatus et Florius Fidelis fecerunt
EDCS-07800444.translation: O. Macrius, a Roman equestrian, lived 20 years. His wife Valeria Frontina and Florius Cogitatus and Florius Fidelis made this.
EDCS-07800444.summary: This is a funerary monument for O. Macrius, a Roman equestrian who died at age 20, erected by his wife Valeria Frontina and two other individuals, Florius Cogitatus and Florius Fidelis, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800448.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aurel[ius/a], aged [---] years.
EDCS-07800448.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Manes, likely for an individual named Aurelius or Aurelia, dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800455.text_edition: Deo Marti Corotiaco Simplicia pro se votum posuit libens merito  Glaucus fecit
EDCS-07800455.translation: To the god Mars Corotiacus, Simplicia willingly and deservedly placed a vow for herself. Glaucus made this.
EDCS-07800455.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Mars Corotiacus by Simplicia for herself, with Glaucus as the craftsman, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800457.text_edition: promisi denarios sex pro voto soluto pecuniam dedi
EDCS-07800457.translation: I promised six denarii for a vow fulfilled, I gave the money.
EDCS-07800457.summary: This is a short, personal inscription recording the payment of six denarii in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800460.text_edition: Deo Marti Alatori Dum Censorinus Gemelli filius votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800460.translation: To the god Mars Alator, D. M. Censorinus, son of Gemellus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800460.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Mars Alator by D. M. Censorinus, son of Gemellus, in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800461.text_edition: Marti Toutati Tiberius Claudius Primus Attii libertus votum solvit libens merito
EDCS-07800461.translation: To Mars Toutatis, Tiberius Claudius Primus, freedman of Attius, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800461.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Mars Toutatis by Tiberius Claudius Primus, a freedman of Attius, in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800463.text_edition: Vetus quomodo sanies signiEficatur#significatur#SIGNEFICATUR Tacita deficta
EDCS-07800463.translation: How old pus is indicated. Tacita, spellbound.
EDCS-07800463.summary: This is an unusual and somewhat enigmatic inscription, possibly a magical or medical text, mentioning 'pus' and 'Tacita', dating to an uncertain period.

EDCS-07800475.text_edition: Dis Manibus sacrum Anicius Saturninus strator consularis monumentum sibi fecit
EDCS-07800475.translation: Sacred to the Divine Manes. Anicius Saturninus, strator consularis, made this monument for himself.
EDCS-07800475.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to the Divine Manes, erected by Anicius Saturninus, a consular groom or stable-master, for himself, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800476.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription, likely funerary or dedicatory, mentioning a name possibly beginning with 'Flav' and the number 25, dating to an uncertain Imperial period.

EDCS-07800477.text_edition: Iovi Optimo Maximo et Numinibus Augustorum Marcus Varius Severus beneficiarius consularis aram cum cancellis de suo posuit
EDCS-07800477.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Divinities of the Augusti, Marcus Varius Severus, a consular beneficiary, placed this altar with railings at his own expense.
EDCS-07800477.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar and railings to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the Augustan Divinities by Marcus Varius Severus, a beneficiary of a consul, indicating a religious offering likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800496.text_edition: Parcis Deabus et Numinibus Augustorum Gaius Antistius Frontinus curator tertium aram de suo dedicavit
EDCS-07800496.translation: To the Goddesses Parcae and the Divinities of the Augusti, Gaius Antistius Frontinus, curator for the third time, dedicated this altar at his own expense.
EDCS-07800496.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an altar to the Parcae Goddesses and the Augustan Divinities by Gaius Antistius Frontinus, a thrice-appointed curator, reflecting a religious vow and civic prominence, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800499.text_edition: Dis Manibus Volusia Faustina civis Lindensis vixit nnos XXVI mensem I dies XXVI Aurelius Senecio decurio ob merita coniugi posuit  Dis Manibus Claudia Catiotui vixtt annos LX
EDCS-07800499.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Volusia Faustina, a citizen of Lindum, lived 26 years, 1 month, 26 days. Aurelius Senecio, a decurion, placed this for his wife on account of her merits. To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudia Catiotui lived 60 years.
EDCS-07800499.summary: This inscription is a funerary monument for Volusia Faustina, erected by her husband Aurelius Senecio, a decurion, and also includes a separate dedication to Claudia Catiotui, indicating a shared or reused monument for two deceased individuals.

EDCS-07800500.text_edition: Dis Manibus Flavius Helius natione Grecus vixit annos XXXX Flavia Ingenua coniugi posuit
EDCS-07800500.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavius Helius, of Greek origin, lived 40 years. Flavia Ingenua placed this for her husband.
