EDCS-69100069.summary: This inscription is a personal letter from Taurus (Taurinus) to his master Macrinus, reporting on a difficult situation involving animals taken by Catarrius and a missed opportunity with Diadumenus, probably dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-69100070.translation: You will give to Titus, aviary-keeper, because throughout the whole forum they boast that they have lent you money. And so I ask you, for your own sake, lest you appear disgraced. You will not love your affairs thus, and you think...
EDCS-69100070.summary: This fragmentary text appears to be part of a private letter or legal document, possibly concerning a debt or a financial dispute and warning the recipient about their reputation.

EDCS-69100071.translation: Atticus, I ask you by bread and salt to send as soon as possible twenty-six denarii in victoriati and ten denarii of Paterionus.
EDCS-69100071.summary: This is a private letter, likely a financial request, from Atticus asking the recipient to send specific sums of money.

EDCS-69100073.translation: to Classicus, prefect of the Sixth Cohort of Nervii.
EDCS-69100073.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Classicus, who served as the prefect of the Sixth Cohort of Nervii, likely from a monument or public work.

EDCS-69100074.translation: ...it is not indeed... Frontinus could have...
EDCS-69100074.summary: This is a small fragment of text, possibly from a letter or narrative, referring to someone named Frontinus and an unfulfilled action.

EDCS-69100076.summary: This is a salutation from a letter or other communication addressed to someone named Carus.

EDCS-69100077.translation: He came to the city eight days before the Kalends of January. Atigniomarus came to receive security for damage and said that he had from you 300 denarii properly, when he asked me.
EDCS-69100077.summary: This text is a private record, possibly a legal or financial note, detailing Atigniomarus's visit to a city to claim security for damages and a sum of money from the recipient.

EDCS-69100078.translation: ...bad thing, that is, to help... they bought a spindle... my lord, brother, with whom they will come as soon as possible from Tincori...
EDCS-69100078.summary: This fragmentary text seems to be part of a private letter or communication, discussing a purchase, assistance, and a future arrival from Tincori.

EDCS-69100079.translation: ...he was in the fort of the Iceni, Epocuria, and Julius Suavis received her/it into his care, and not...
EDCS-69100079.summary: This fragmentary inscription describes a situation involving someone at the fort of the Iceni named Epocuria, who was taken in by Julius Suavis.

EDCS-69100084.translation: When Nero Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus was consul for the second time, and Lucius Calpurnius Piso was consul, on the 6th day before the Ides of January, I, Tibullus, freedman of Venustus, wrote and declare that I owe Gratus, freedman of Spurius, 105 denarii from the price of merchandise which was sold and delivered, which money I ought to return to him or to whomever that matter pertains.
EDCS-69100084.summary: This is a formal legal document, dated to AD 60, recording a debt acknowledged by Tibullus, freedman of Venustus, to Gratus, freedman of Spurius, for merchandise sold.

EDCS-69100085.translation: When Publius Marius Celsus and Lucius Afinius Gallus were consuls, twelve days before the Kalends of November, Marcus Rennius Venustus declares that he hired himself out to Gaius Valerius Proculus to transport twenty loads of provisions from Londinium to Verulamium by the Ides of November, with a transport fee of one quadrans per denarius for each load, on the condition that for any delay caused by me, one as per denarius in Londinium, but if the whole arm...
EDCS-69100085.summary: This is a contractual agreement, dated to October 21st, AD 62, in which Marcus Rennius Venustus details his hire by Gaius Valerius Proculus to transport provisions between Londinium and Verulamium, including terms regarding payment and penalties for delay.

EDCS-69100090.translation: In the consulship of Marcus Licinius Crassus Frugi and Gaius Laecanius Bassus, on the [day] of September, Florentinus, a slave of Sextus Cassius [...], wrote, by order of my master, that he received two payments from the dedicated fund...
EDCS-69100090.summary: This inscription records a payment received from a dedicated fund, dated to 64 AD by the names of the consuls, and was written by a slave on behalf of his master.

EDCS-69100091.translation: In the consulship of Imperator Caesar Vespasian for the 7th time and Titus for the 5th time, on the 11th Kalends of November (October 22nd), work for the Ides of November (November 13th) was given between Litugenus and Magunus by Caesar as a preliminary judgment.
EDCS-69100091.summary: This inscription records a preliminary judgment regarding work to be performed, dated to 76 AD by the consuls Vespasian and Titus.

EDCS-69100092.translation: In the consulship of Imperator Domitian Caesar Augustus Germanicus...
EDCS-69100092.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dating clause, indicating the consulship of Emperor Domitian Augustus Germanicus, likely between 83 and 96 AD.

EDCS-69100093.translation: In the consulship of Imperator Caesar Domitian Augustus for the 8th time, on the Ides of March (March 15th), Communis wrote that he owed what...
EDCS-69100093.summary: This inscription records a declaration of debt made by Communis, dated to March 15th, 92 AD, during Domitian's eighth consulship.

EDCS-69100096.translation: ... promised on faith to be given ... of the principal or interest under ... case ... home investigation ... Rusticus who ... singular ... money ... of the district.
EDCS-69100096.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a legal or financial document, mentioning promises of payment, interest, and the names Rusticus and possibly a military unit or a local district.

EDCS-69100097.translation: ... to conduct and pursue all his affairs, to make a stipulation, to contend in court, you permitted; likewise, he, being present, managed the matter and the procuration for the freeborn...
EDCS-69100097.summary: This fragmentary inscription details legal permissions granted to someone to manage their affairs, make stipulations, and contend in court, possibly related to the legal standing of freeborn individuals.

EDCS-69100098.summary: This is a fragment likely listing names, possibly part of a list of officials, soldiers, or other individuals.

EDCS-69100099.translation: To Attius Optatus, stealer, at Durobrivae.
EDCS-69100099.summary: This inscription addresses Attius Optatus as a 'stealer' (furator) and indicates his location as Durobrivae, possibly a legal accusation or a public denouncement.

EDCS-69100101.translation: ... money ... loan ... Primus ... Billiccus ... Vannii ... callulxiuci ... decurions ...
EDCS-69100101.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary list related to a monetary loan, mentioning several individuals and 'decurions,' suggesting a local municipal context.

EDCS-69100102.translation: ... of the Britons ... but ... received ... old ... troop ... Longinus, troop of Mar... Agrippa, troop of Silvanus; Verecundus, troop of Silvanus ...
EDCS-69100102.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions 'Britons' and lists soldiers, including Longinus, Agrippa, and Verecundus, along with their respective 'troops' (turmae), indicating a military context.

EDCS-69100103.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of personal names, possibly recording individuals or family members.

EDCS-69100106.summary: This fragmentary inscription preserves two personal names, likely part of a longer list or dedication.

EDCS-69100107.translation: Caesar's [son/heir/legate?], from Iun[...], Mansuetus, Sextus, Neon, Aristis.
EDCS-69100107.summary: This fragmented inscription seems to list several individuals, possibly linked to a Caesar, and may record family or official connections.

EDCS-69100108.summary: This short inscription records the name of Tiberius Aprilius, likely identifying an individual.

EDCS-69100110.translation: Catullus [...], it is made [for/by?] a slave, 16 denarii and a quadrant. Catullus, a slave of Romanius(?) Faustinus, 2065 denarii. A slave of Senecio(?), 6 denarii.
EDCS-69100110.summary: This inscription is an account recording payments or values associated with individuals, including slaves, and their owners, indicating monetary transactions.

EDCS-69100112.translation: Account of Crispus: beer 5 denarii. [...] 105 [units/items?], 7 denarii. 3 asses for beer through Butus, half a denarius. [...] 51 denarii and asses [...]. Beer 2 denarii and asses; beer half a denarius and a quadrant. Through Ianuarius, beer 1 denarius and a half. From that...
EDCS-69100112.summary: This inscription is a detailed account of expenditures, primarily for beer, listing quantities and prices, and names of individuals involved in the transactions, indicating daily commercial activities.

EDCS-69100116.translation: 30 denarii Ammonicus owes. Exuvius(?). Pactumeius, son of Adenhes. From the total sum of denarii to whom...
EDCS-69100116.summary: This inscription appears to be a financial record, noting a debt owed by Ammonicus and mentioning other individuals, possibly related to a transaction or a larger account.

EDCS-69200355.translation: Quintus, M., L., freedman of Quintus. To Oliva, [sweets?] 6 pounds.
EDCS-69200355.summary: This inscription identifies a freedman named Quintus and records a gift or sale of six pounds of sweets to Oliva.

EDCS-69200359.summary: This short inscription records the name Gaius Atisius, likely identifying an individual.

EDCS-69200362.summary: This inscription records the full name of Quintus Attius Crescens, identifying an individual.

EDCS-71300115.translation: On the first day of the Ides of July, by Lucretio[nis].
EDCS-71300115.summary: This inscription records a date, July 1st, and potentially the name of an individual, Lucretio, likely marking a specific event or transaction.

EDCS-71300121.translation: Antoni, may you live in God.
EDCS-71300121.summary: This is a Christian funerary inscription expressing a wish for Antoni to live in God, indicating a belief in an afterlife.

EDCS-71300137.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely dedicated to the Emperor Maximian, possibly from a monument or public work dating to his reign (late 3rd/early 4th century AD).

EDCS-71300141.translation: To Fortuna Redux, Eltaominus, veteran, formerly an architect of the ala Vettonum, willingly, gladly, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-71300141.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Fortuna Redux by Eltaominus, a veteran and former architect of the Ala Vettonum cavalry unit, in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-71300143.translation: Aurelia Toisia, Aurelius Marinus, Aurelius Marianus. Lupia lived 25 years. Pannoni lived 5 years. Vitalis lived 5 years, and Saturnin[us].
EDCS-71300143.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription commemorating several individuals, including Aurelia Toisia, Aurelius Marinus, Aurelius Marianus, Lupia, Pannoni, Vitalis, and Saturninus, providing their names and ages at death for some.

EDCS-72200126.summary: This is a very brief and fragmentary inscription likely referencing a family or group named Aelii, possibly indicating ownership or dedication.

EDCS-72200129.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or memorial, mentioning the name Alioni Securio.

EDCS-72200130.summary: This inscription lists two pairs of names, possibly indicating individuals involved in a transaction, a dedication, or members of a family group.

EDCS-72200131.translation: Broccus? Decimus G. S. Aspis M. 10238.
EDCS-72200131.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to list several names, including Broccus and Decimus G. S., along with a name Aspis followed by a large number, which might indicate a measurement, quantity, or another form of identification.

EDCS-72200133.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription naming Adiutus Florescens, possibly part of a dedication, funerary monument, or record of an individual.

EDCS-72200134.summary: This inscription records two abbreviated Roman names, likely indicating ownership or dedication on a small object.

EDCS-72200138.summary: This inscription lists two personal names, Monimus and an abbreviated name possibly Musaeus, likely indicating ownership or craftsmanship.

EDCS-72200140.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius, followed by two abbreviated names, possibly indicating ownership or a dedicatory act.

EDCS-72200142.summary: This inscription presents a fragmentary first line followed by the name Brocchus, likely marking ownership or a craftsman's signature.

EDCS-72600242.summary: This inscription marks an object as originating from the workshop of Saturninus, likely indicating a manufacturer's stamp.

EDCS-72600243.summary: This inscription indicates that the object was produced in the workshop of Murranus, serving as a manufacturer's mark.

EDCS-72600245.summary: This inscription identifies the object as coming from the workshop of Niger, functioning as a brand or origin mark.

EDCS-72600248.summary: This inscription, identical in meaning to EDCS-72600245, specifies that the object originated from the workshop of Niger.

EDCS-72600249.summary: This inscription records that Secundus was the maker of the object, indicating a craftsman's signature.

EDCS-72600250.summary: This inscription identifies the object as being from the factory of Andegenus, likely a manufacturing stamp.

EDCS-72600251.summary: This inscription records the name of the workshop owner, Aper, likely indicating the origin of a manufactured item.

EDCS-72600253.summary: This inscription identifies Albucius as the maker or craftsman of the object on which it is inscribed.

EDCS-72600254.summary: This inscription attributes the work to Marcellus, specifying his direct involvement in its creation.

EDCS-72600257.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop of Passenus as the origin of the inscribed object.

EDCS-72600258.summary: This inscription indicates that the item originated from Mansuetus's workshop.

EDCS-72600262.summary: This inscription records Paternus as the maker or craftsman of the object.

EDCS-72600263.summary: This inscription indicates that the item originated from Atilianus's workshop.

EDCS-72600269.summary: This inscription indicates that the item originated from Iulus's workshop.

EDCS-72600273.summary: This inscription identifies Maccius as the maker or craftsman of the object.

EDCS-72600385.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop of Reburrus as the origin of the inscribed object.

EDCS-72700165.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Bodicacia, his wife, lived 27 years.
EDCS-72700165.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Bodicacia by her husband, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-72700166.summary: This brief inscription likely marks a tomb or monument for Domnica while she was still living, indicating it was prepared in advance.

EDCS-72900031.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-72900031.translation: The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, acclaimed imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, granted Roman citizenship to the cavalrymen and infantrymen of the army of Germania Pia Fidelis who served in 4 alae and 14 cohorts (which are called Noricorum and Sulpicia and Afrorum Veterana and I Thracum and I Flavia Hispanorum and I Latobicorum and Varcianorum and I Pannoniorum and Dalmatarum and II c. R. and I Raetorum and VI Brittonum Pia Fidelis and II Asturum Pia Fidelis and I Classica Pia Fidelis and III and VI Breucorum and I Lucensium Pia Fidelis and II Varcianorum and VI Raetorum and IV Thracum) and are in Germania Inferior under Salvius Iulianus, having served twenty-five years, and likewise to the marines, having served twenty-six years, having been honorably discharged, whose names are written below. He gave Roman citizenship to those of them who did not have it, and the right of marriage with the wives whom they then had when citizenship was given to them, or with those whom they afterwards married, provided they were single. On the 13th day before the Kalends of December, when Gaius Curtius Iustus and Gaius Iulius Iulianus were consuls. From a marine of the Germanic Fleet Pia Fidelis, commanded by Marcus Ulpius Ulpianus: Velvotigernus, son of Magiotigernus, a Briton. Transcribed and checked from the bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the temple of the deified Augustus, near Minerva. The Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 13th time, acclaimed imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, granted to the cavalrymen and infantrymen of the army Pia Fidelis who served in 4 alae and 14 cohorts and are in Germania Inferior under Salvius Iulianus, having served twenty-five years, and likewise to the marines, having served twenty-six years, having been honorably discharged, whose names are written below, Roman citizenship to those of them who did not have it, and the right of marriage with the wives whom they then had when citizenship was given to them, or with those whom they afterwards married. On the 13th day before the Kalends of December, when Iustus and Iulianus were consuls. From a marine of the Germanic Fleet Pia Fidelis, commanded by Marcus Ulpius Ulpianus: Velvotigernus, son of Magiotigernus, a Briton. [Witnessed by] Marcus Servilius Geta, Lucius Pullius Chresimus, Marcus Sentilius Iasus, Tiberius Iulius Felix, Gaius Iulius Silvanus, Lucius Pullius Velox, Publius Ocilius Priscus.
EDCS-72900031.summary: This is a Roman military diploma, issued by Emperor Antoninus Pius in 150 CE, granting Roman citizenship and the right of legal marriage to auxiliary soldiers and marines from the army of Germania Inferior, specifically for a marine named Velvotigernus, son of Magiotigernus, a Briton.

EDCS-72900124.translation: Of the two Emperors, Augusti, Antoninus and Verus, conquerors of Armenia.
EDCS-72900124.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the two co-emperors, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Verus, celebrating their victory in the Parthian War (c. 161-166 CE), which earned them the title 'Armeniacus'.

EDCS-73900074.summary: This inscription appears to be a potter's stamp or a similar maker's mark, indicating production from the workshop of Patricius and possibly a product name or type 'Solita'.

EDCS-73900079.summary: This is a maker's inscription, likely a potter's stamp, indicating that Albinus produced the object at Lugdunum (modern Lyon).

EDCS-73900084.summary: This is a brief maker's mark, probably a potter's stamp, indicating that Maximus was the creator of the object.

EDCS-73900094.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely a name or part of a dedication, belonging to Quintus Valerius.

EDCS-73900103.summary: This short inscription, similar to another in the collection, likely indicates a pre-prepared monument or grave for Domnica while she was still alive.

EDCS-75300138.translation: Born to Mercury, to the Genius who stole or will steal an amulet from me: may holy Genius, if a female slave, if a free person, if a woman, if a boy, if a girl... permit him... may he lose his modesty... may he... with his foot... may he not eat, nor sit, nor relieve himself, unless he represents it at your temple. May he obtain.
EDCS-75300138.summary: This is a defixio or curse tablet, invoking Mercury and a personal Genius to punish a thief who stole an amulet, detailing a series of misfortunes the thief should suffer until the item is returned or confession is made at the deity's temple.

EDCS-76900067.translation: From Nero Caesar Augustus Britannicus, spread from Magul with works in the province, under Trebellius Maximus, legate of Augustus.
EDCS-76900067.summary: This inscription records the expansion of works in a province, possibly related to water distribution, under the authority of Emperor Nero and his legate Trebellius Maximus.

EDCS-78300031.translation: Of the two Augusti, Antoninus and Verus, Armenian.
EDCS-78300031.summary: This inscription commemorates the two Augusti, Antoninus and Verus, acknowledging their titles as 'Armenian', likely dating to the period of their joint reign after the Parthian War (161-169 AD).

EDCS-78300053.summary: This is a simple two-line inscription, likely a dedication or ownership mark, featuring two names: Giamillus and Atticus Nico.

EDCS-78300655.translation: [And] the principal and their interest which he owed, properly and correctly to be managed whenever it is sought. They [---] to Narcissus, to Rogatus Lingon, faithfully promised to be given correctly and properly. Atticus [---] to Ingenuus [---]ILLIS, or to whomever that matter pertains, and those whom the aforesaid persons declared in front of them that they owe and possess and had received before this day. Atticus [---]
EDCS-78300655.summary: This inscription records a legal or financial agreement concerning a debt, principal, and interest, with Atticus making a promise of payment to Narcissus, Rogatus Lingon, and Ingenuus, indicating a contractual obligation.
